data.title: Alibaba's AI Ambitions Are Promising, But There Are Better AI Stocks
data.description:
Alibaba Group Holding Limited (NYSE:BABA)’s stock corrected ~7% immediately after March earnings, and it is still down more than 7% at the time of writing. Despite this, I believe Alibaba’s earnings were quite good, showing early signs of a turnaround and the start of a possible new growth story.

I think the market overreacted to a relatively meaningless miss on both top and bottom line. Financial results are not the issue with BABA, in my view. The problem is, as always, the omnipresent political risk tied to investing in China.

With rising tensions between China, the US and the West, and an increasingly polarized world, I believe there is still too much risk to invest in speculative Chinese companies - it does not matter whether they are cheap according to traditional valuation metrics. While Alibaba’s results are promising I think there are far better and safer AI stocks in the market.

Quarterly earnings show the early signs of a turnaround

For the first time since I started my coverage of Alibaba, I finally noticed some promising signs of business turnaround in March Quarterly earnings. The company was struggling until recently, in my view, with flat eCommerce growth and sub-par growth in Cloud when compared to peers.

Finally, Alibaba is showing signs of timid business acceleration. Overall revenue is growing at 7%, up from the low single digits registered for the past few quarters. Taobao and Tmall, the core eCommerce business of BABA, are growing at 9%, while Cloud accelerated to 18% year-on-year.

I do not think these figures are impressive just yet. A supposed tech company in the AI space, in my view, should showcase far higher growth rates - especially for what concerns its Cloud division. However, I do recognize that the company is finally showing signs of a turnaround and could build an interesting growth story if management can grow from here.

I believe that Alibaba’s growth in Cloud - now a key revenue driver - can be attributed to the overall acceleration in demand for AI solutions. This is benefiting a vast array of companies, and BABA has been able to capture some of that demand. This is what I will cover next.

How Alibaba is playing the AI race

As a keen observer of the AI space, I have noted multiple times in my work on Seeking Alpha how one of the ultimate objectives of AI is to replace human input in the labor market. This, however, will not be a linear process.

I believe AI to be a phenomenon that comes in waves, with different applications at different stages. The image above outlines how AI is coming at different “levels”, starting with relatively limited Chatbot and text-based solutions and evolving until a future where AI can take over the entire workforce in a company.

I believe we are currently between Level 2 and Level 3 of the chart above, rapidly evolving to the stage of AI agents, which are systems that can already automate a set of actions that may normally take one or more employees to perform. Many public companies are already rolling-out AI agents - something I will cover in my next section and I expect we should enter fully Level 3 in the next months.

In the table below, I summarized some of Alibaba’s current product offering for what concerns AI solutions, with comments about what the product is exactly and whether it is monetized, and how.

Product Name

Description

Monetization

Tongyi Qianwen (Qwen Series)

A family of large language models (LLMs) including Qwen-Max, Qwen-Plus and other Qwen LLMs, supporting text generation, multimodal tasks, coding, and reasoning.

Monetized through Alibaba Cloud’s Platform via API call fees, model training, and deployment service charges.

Tongyi Wanxiang

A generative AI model for image generation using diffusion technology, enhanced by Alibaba’s Composer model based on text prompts.

Monetized through Alibaba Cloud’s Modelscope via SaaS fees for businesses.

Wan 2.1-FLF2V-14B

An AI video generation model supporting text-to-image and text-to-video generation, released in April 2025.

Not monetized; offered free on Alibaba Cloud to drive cloud service subscriptions.

Quanzhantui

AI-powered marketing solution enhancing merchants’ ad campaign efficiency and targeting on Taobao and Tmall.

Monetized through software service fees charged to merchants on Taobao and Tmall.

AI-Powered Sourcing Engine

A conversational B2B sourcing tool launched by AIDC in September 2024, matching buyers with suppliers using voice or text prompts.

Monetized through transaction fees and platform service charges on Alibaba’s international platforms.

Lingma

A coding copilot powered by Qwen models, offering intelligent code generation, multi-file code modification, and AI chat for developers.

Monetized via usage-based fees for developers on Alibaba Cloud.

AliMe

An AI-powered customer service chatbot using speech recognition and deep learning, handling over 2 million daily sessions on platforms like Taobao.

Monetized indirectly by reducing human customer service costs on Alibaba platforms.

E-commerce Brain

A system using real-time data to predict consumer preferences and provide personalized product recommendations on Taobao and Tmall.

Monetized through advertising fees from merchants and higher conversion rates.

Ali Smart Supply Chain (ASSC)

An AI-driven tool for supply chain management, forecasting demand, optimizing inventory, and determining pricing strategies.

Monetized through service fees charged to merchants using Alibaba’s platforms.

City Brain

A cloud-based AI system for urban management, optimizing traffic flow and city operations, reducing traffic jams by 15% in Hangzhou.

Monetized through contracts and service fees from municipal governments.

Tmall Smart Selection

An AI algorithm using deep learning to recommend products and communicate inventory needs to retailers during high-traffic events.

Monetized through merchant fees for platform services and increased sales from optimized recommendations.

Platform for AI [PAI]

A machine learning platform offering data preprocessing, model training, deployment, and evaluation tools like PAI-Designer.

Monetized through subscription and usage-based fees for cloud-based AI development services.

Modelscope

A SaaS-like platform providing access to pre-trained generative AI models for businesses to develop custom applications.

Monetized through service fees for access to models and cloud infrastructure on Alibaba Cloud.

Overall, I think Alibaba is playing a good game in the AI space, with monetization starting to come through mostly via their “Cloud” division, which includes most of the AI applications listed above.

However, an 18% year-on-year growth on Cloud is not what I would like to see for a leader in the AI space. Based on March Quarter figures of 9% revenue growth, Alibaba’s PE/G ratio is about 1.4X. Based on earnings growth of ~23%, Alibaba's PE/G ratio is ~0.5X.

While reasonable in absolute terms, other established companies are registering similar growth, while having little political risk in them. Together with exposure to the China domestic market, this less-than-impressive AI growth is the reason why I am maintaining a SELL rating on the stock. I ultimately think there are better AI plays out there.

A note about shares buybacks and PE/G ratios

Alibaba bulls often mention stock repurchases as one of the key reasons why the company is worthy of an investment. Management has been vocal about how much they care about buybacks. In March earnings, the second page of the presentation was fully dedicated to stock repurchases. The company bought back more than 5% of shares in its last fiscal year.

Personally, I would prefer to see management focusing on accelerating a turnaround of the business, rather than deploying so many resources on buybacks. Ultimately, I do not think any company can “repurchase itself” to greatness. Without a thriving core business, buybacks are pointless, in my view. I think a true bull case in Alibaba can only manifest if its political risk vanishes or gets significantly reduced (something I will cover in my risk section).

For what concerns Alibaba's PE/G ratio, I have reported this figure both based on revenue and on earnings growth. Traditionally, a PE/G ratio takes into consideration earnings growth, not revenue growth- and Alibaba is indeed very cheap based on earnings growth at 0.5X (vs. 1.4X on revenue). However, I believe both figures should be considered when evaluating an investment in the AI space. Being a CAPEX-intense sector, I think a PE/G ratio based on revenue trails ahead of one based on earnings growth. As AI companies invest heavily in AI (which has a negative effect on earnings and cash flow in the short term), AI-fueled revenue growth manifests before earnings growth. Considering both figures, I believe, provides a better overall picture on the business.

What AI stocks I would pick over Alibaba

I see plenty of options for investors that want to gain exposure to the AI sector. Alibaba, in my view, is not one of the best, even net of the political risk tied to investing in China and the weakness of the domestic Chinese market.

As this article is based on Alibaba, I won’t spend too much time explaining how other companies are, in my view, better playing the AI race. I will however list three companies that showcase similar or higher growth figures to BABA but have products and investment thesis that are in my opinion better than those of Alibaba. For the sake of a fair comparison, I will also limit myself to companies that are mature, as Alibaba is, and do not represent high risk, asymmetric investment bets.

Starting with NVIDIA Corporations (NVDA), growing overall revenue at a staggering 78% year-on-year (Seeking Alpha data, Q1 2025) and currently valued at a PE/G ratio of ~0.4X on revenue and ~1.3X based on earnings growth. Nvidia’s core business is providing what I think are the “picks and shovels” of the AI race (GPUs), and it is one of the main companies that first benefits from AI demand.

While Nvidia is exposed to China, I see its “China risk” as not comparable to that of Alibaba in the slightest. First and foremost, the company is American based. Secondly, it sells globally and China only represents about $ 15 Billion in sales for the company. Nvidia’s very reasonable PE/G valuation makes it for me a no brainer pick over Alibaba.

Another AI pick I would prefer over Alibaba is Microsoft Corporation (MSFT), which I covered recently, analyzing its balance sheet. The company is more expensive than Alibaba, at a PE/G ratio of ~2.6X based on last quarter’s 13% revenue growth and a FWD P/E of ~34x (Seeking Alpha data), and ~2.3X based on earnings growth. However, I see this premium more than justified by the fact that Microsoft is not a Chinese company, as well as because Microsoft is partnering with Open AI and has a huge moat in terms of rolling up and monetizing AI, thanks to Windows, Office and AWS. While Nvidia is cheaper than BABA and far less risky, Microsoft is a company with an exceptional track record which I believe deserves its premium valuation over BABA.

The last AI stock I would prefer over Alibaba is Alphabet, Inc. (GOOGL), which I also covered recently. Despite assigning a HOLD rating to this stock, and not having direct exposure to it in my portfolio, I still prefer it over Alibaba. I think Google shares one characteristic with Alibaba: it is cheap. At a FWD P/E of just below ~18 at the time of writing (Seeking Alpha data), and 12% Q1 revenue growth, the company features a PE/G ratio of just 1.5X based on revenue, and 1.1X based on earnings growth. In my view, the market discounts Google because of the AI threat to its Search cash cow. Personally, I would rather bet on Google managing to fend off this threat, than on the Chinese economy somehow opening to the West and political risk in China to permanently decrease. In short, if readers want a cheap AI stock, I would rather recommend Google than Alibaba.

Risks to my thesis

Since I have started my Seeking Alpha coverage on Alibaba, I have always argued how the “elephant in the room” with an investment in this company is political risk. The main risk to my thesis, today as always, is that the Chinese Communist Party pivots to a more open political and economic stance towards the West. More broadly, any news related to de-escalating tensions between China and the West (even if initiated by the West) could also act like a catalyst and trigger a stock rally.

Should political risk in China disappear or significantly reduce, I think there would be a major bull market for all Chinese stocks. Alibaba, as a prominent Tech stock, would benefit disproportionately, in my view.

Other risks to my bear thesis include general volatility and turning market sentiment. Alibaba performed decently in the past few months, albeit below the Nasdaq and only after a prolonged bear market (the company still sits ~60% below its ATH in 2021). While the fundamentals of the company (and political risk) did not change, market sentiment together with significant shares buybacks still managed to sustain BABA’s share price in the last months.

Conclusion

Alibaba has been fascinating investors for years because of how “cheap” it is. Buying a company just because it is cheap is, in my view, generally a mistake. However, Alibaba bulls also tend to argue that the company is not only cheap, but it is also an emerging global contender in the AI race. That, I recognize after March earnings, is true.

The problem is that I believe there are far better AI stocks in the market. Companies that are cheaper or similarly priced as Alibaba, but that, contrary to Alibaba, have no (or limited) political risk tied to them. Companies that, contrary to Alibaba, have a stronger track record of double digit growth and rolling out AI-related innovation to customers.

If readers are fascinated by the cheap valuation of Alibaba, I recommend they rather look at Nvidia, which is trading at a discount relative to its history and it is a proven AI company with an outstanding track record.

If readers are intrigued by Alibaba’s AI-fueled Cloud growth, I recommend they rather look at Microsoft or Google, both big global players in the Cloud space. In the case of Google, readers can also find a “comeback” story similar to that of Alibaba - with the difference that said comeback can happen as a result of management’s execution and not politics.

Personally, I continue assigning a “SELL” rating to BABA. I do not see a company that can generate alpha, as it remains entangled in political risk in an ever more polarized world. While it’s showing promising results in AI, I think there are cheaper and/or better AI companies to invest in.

data.title: LKQ Corporation: Quietly Crushing It Again
data.description:
Summary

I’m going to start off by cutting to the chase - LKQ Corporation (NASDAQ:LKQ) still put up some solid numbers last quarter. Their size and market positioning are really helping them out in both North America and Europe. Even though the whole industry might face some profit challenges down the road, I think LKQ still looks pretty good at current price levels when you consider their strong history and size. They're also making smart moves with acquisitions and using tech to stay competitive. Plus, they're giving back to shareholders with dividends and buybacks, which is why I'm sticking with a Buy rating. The key thesis points I made in my original article still hold and as a reminder they include:

  • Undervalued from a historic basis

  • Strong historical financial performance and balance sheet

  • Promising growth prospects based on a size and scale advantage

There Is Still Financial Performance and Revenue Growth, Based On What We Can See

In 1Q25, LKQ showed us they can still put out solid numbers despite a rapidly changing environment, particularly in North America. Both North American and European segments benefited from organic growth initiatives and strategic market positioning, favorable FX rates helped boost LKQ's top-line numbers, contributing to overall revenue growth. On a per share basis, revenue still look very solid for what you get.

“North America also benefited from having a diversified portfolio of products and services, mitigating potential shortfalls in other business areas. We generated positive growth for our lead tech, calibration, and diagnostics business, and our bumper-to-bumper hard parts business in Canada.” - Justin Jude - President and CEO

LKQ currently maintains respectable profit margins while expanding market share, although future periods could look uglier. What does this mean for LKQ stock? I’ll elaborate more once I go through my model assumption changes and valuation methodology, later in this article. However, if you’re unwilling to scroll down further, here is what you need to know - the swift changes underpinning the industry are going to be an overall profit killer - I know this, you know this, the market knows this. The market does tend to overreact and I believe the price is now very attractive relative to where performance is expected to land for the rest of FY25. Things are changing so fast that when I first started writing this, things looked pretty grim and then we got the auto sector tariff exemption. That doesn’t change my view that LKQ is a solid performer, is well run and possesses a very valuable size and scale advantage.

Strategic Acquisitions and Market Expansion

LKQ isn't merely aiming for incremental gains but is and has been pursuing a strategy that leverages its size and scale advantage. Strategic acquisitions have been a component of LKQ's growth trajectory, contributing to revenue growth, although not all growth is organic. These are often calculated decisions, aimed at expanding the LKQ's operational capabilities and strengthening its supply chain.

By integrating strategic acquisitions, LKQ can improve its market position and strengthen its competitive edge, potentially making life harder for competitors. To improve overall efficiency and enhance products, management is surgically integrating these new components. By doing this, LKQ strengthens its competitive position, making it harder for others to compete.

Technological Advancements and Innovation

Technology is a part of enhancing operational capabilities and keeping a competitive edge, a theme observed across various companies and is not specific to LKQ. It's common for companies to invest in technology and digital platforms to boost efficiency. Arguments can be made whether this will boost LKQ's position or maintain it, but the fact that they're doing it keeps them in the game relative to others in the space. Staying proactive in technological advancements is essential for long-term sustainability and growth.

Thinking Optimistically, The Stock Price Can Still Go Up If Earnings Underperform Because……

LKQ has historically returned value in other ways via a combination of dividends and stock repurchases. In 1Q25, LKQ declared a quarterly cash dividend of $0.30 per share, payable on March 27th, and subsequently declared another dividend of the same amount payable on May 29th. Additionally, LKQ has been actively repurchasing its shares, with a total of ~1 million shares repurchased in 1Q25. LKQ still has a substantial authorization remaining for future repurchases, with approximately $1.7B available through October 2026.

For 1Q25 Earnings Estimates, I Give Myself A B+

As you can see above, my 1Q estimate was optimistic. The world was a lot different back when I last updated this model, and for being +/-10% within print with all that has gone on in the past few months is nothing short of a miracle, in my opinion. That also means management guides pretty well, so I can reasonably follow what management spews out as a forecasting benchmark.

Above is a snippet of my estimates, where I tried to follow towards the low end of management guidance. I didn’t quite get to management’s guide and I think the assumptions I have in there currently are reasonable. We also now have a bridge to adjusted EPS figures! I’m not a big fan of modeling to an adjusted figure, but that is the number cited by management and leads to a Who’s on First bit if you don’t at least measure it - I’m using it as a sense check for rest of 2025 assumptions. As I’ve noted in my other pieces, I tend to model conservatively and look for ways to break my thesis and assumptions, as opposed to making outlandish claims (TSLA $3,000 price target, anyone?).

We Are Undervalued Territory

When I first wrote about LKQ, my price target was in the high $60s. As I’ve mentioned here and in other articles, boy was the world much different then. Nonetheless, the number I’m getting to is now somewhere in the high $50s based on the changes in assumptions. Had I actually been able to get to management’s guidance figures, the stock should be valued a little higher. All in all, given current trading levels in the $40s, pricing looks very attractive today.

Conclusion: Reiterate Buy Rating

To conclude, LKQ can still demonstrate ongoing financial strength and maintaining revenues, supported by their strategic advantages in size and scale. Their proactive approach to market expansion through acquisitions and investments in technology could serve as a growth catalyst if the conditions remain favorable. Coupled with a consistent return of value to shareholders via dividends and share repurchases, I maintain a positive outlook on LKQ's stock, reinforcing a Buy recommendation despite known industry headwinds and further potential volatility.

One final thing I’ll leave you with - Seeking Alpha has a new nifty seasonality feature you can use to time your stock purchases and sales, if you do that sort of thing.

data.title: German Ifo Index Increases In May
data.description:
By Carsten Brzeski

German businesses remain cautiously optimistic. After the back-and-forth on US tariffs, German businesses seem to be focusing on the bright side of what could happen under the new German government, rather than fearing the downsides from ongoing uncertainty and trade tensions. This is, at least, the message that the just-released Ifo index seems to convey. Germany's most prominent leading indicator improved again and came in at 87.3 in May, from 86.9 in April. While the current assessment component weakened to 86.1, from 86.4 in April, expectations improved significantly to 88.9, from 87.4, the highest in a year.

Between nearer-term risks and longer-term opportunities

Taking a step back from high frequency data (and noise), the German economy remains in the middle of two seismic activities: a new government, which seems to lack strong ambition for structural reforms but will have access to unprecedented fiscal space for infrastructure and defence investments, and fundamental shifts in trade and geopolitics, including US tariffs. We think that, at least in the shorter run, the negatives will outweigh the positives. Not that we enjoy negative news, but we're currently witnessing several official forecasts for German growth this year converging with our long-held view of yet another year of stagnation. Yesterday’s forecasts from the Germany council of economic advisors was just another example.

Still, not everything is bad. The German economy actually grew in the first quarter, probably as a result of export frontloading, and industrial data pointed to some cyclical rebound at least before 'Liberation Day'. The tariff extravaganza since the beginning of April, however, will leave clear marks on the German economy. There is the direct impact, as even with the current 90-days-pause, tariffs are still higher than at the start of the year, but also the indirect impact via confidence and still high uncertainty. In the longer term, the announced fiscal stimulus will boost growth in Germany. Implemented in the right way, investment in infrastructure should lead to a cyclical upswing, at least. The caveat, however, remains that the fiscal measures alone – impressive as they might be – will do very little to improve the economy’s competitiveness. Modern infrastructure is essential for one of the world's largest economies, but it doesn't inherently drive innovation, sector transformation, or new growth opportunities. In this regard, we still expect tensions within the government about potential expenditure cuts and some structural reforms as the intended additional spending plans, other than infrastructure and defence, are hard to reconcile with the European fiscal rules.

The frustrating observation, however, is that the analysis of a stagnating economy with the need for investment and structural change is not new. While the discussion has been going on for years now, actual action to change the situation has been limited. In fact, due to the tensions within the last government, the elections and the new government just off the starting blocks, another year of economic policy standstill has passed. Last week, Chancellor Friedrich Merz announced that Germans should feel some positive changes by this summer. We agree that a quick implementation of policy and quick results will be needed but remain sceptical that the summer will bring significant change given the long implementation lags of public investment. Rather than seeing tangible improvements, Germans may find themselves noticing little more than old fax machines still lingering on the streets of Berlin. Public investment and structural changes simply take time.

All in all, today’s Ifo index comes as a positive surprise. Still, we caution against overly hasty optimism. There are currently more unknowns than knowns for the German economy, and we continue to expect another year of stagnation – which would mark the first time ever for Germany to go through three consecutive years without growth.

Content Disclaimer

This publication has been prepared by ING solely for information purposes irrespective of a particular user's means, financial situation or investment objectives. The information does not constitute investment recommendation, and nor is it investment, legal or tax advice or an offer or solicitation to purchase or sell any financial instrument. Read more.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: How This Income ETF Provides Investors With Monthly Payouts
data.description:
Follow GPIQ here

This video's transcript was generated by a third party. It is not curated or reviewed and is provided for convenience and information purposes only. The accuracy and completeness of the transcript are not guaranteed.

Past performance is no guarantee of future results. Content is offered for information purposes only. Unless stated otherwise, any and all individuals participating in the video are third parties that include both professional investors and individual investors who may not be licensed or certified by any institute or regulatory body.

Unless stated otherwise, the views or opinions expressed may not reflect those of Seeking Alpha as a whole. The accuracy and completeness of content shared cannot be guaranteed. Seeking Alpha does not take account of your objectives or financial situation and does not offer any personalized investment advice. Seeking Alpha is not a licensed securities dealer, broker, U.S. investment adviser, or investment bank.

Daniel Snyder: Hey, everyone, Daniel Snyder here from Seeking Alpha. This is another episode of ETF Spotlight.

Today, we have a great treat for all of you seeking income, but also love the tech focused space as well as others, because we are joined today by Sirion Skulpone, who is joining us as Co-Head of Client Portfolio Management for Wealth Investment Solutions at Goldman Sachs. And we have to talk about this because this is the interesting space of tracking the indexes but also using that derivative side of getting income, which I know a lot of people are focused on.

So, Sirion, thank you so much for joining us today. I was hoping we could kick things off. The GPIQ fund that you have over there at Goldman Sachs tracks the NASDAQ 100 and has the derivatives aspect to it. But just wondering if you might be able to dive a little bit deeper into how the fund is put together as well as how investors might be able to utilize this within a portfolio.

Sirion Skulpone: Sure. Well, thanks for having me. Really excited to be here to talk about GPIQ.

So GPIQ is what's broadly known as a derivative income ETF. So there's different flavors of these, but generally, this refers to strategies that invest in stocks, as you said, and then sell options in order to generate income.

So you might also have heard of these as premium income, options overlay, covered call, buy-write strategies. That's a term that we use a lot. So generally a buy-write strategy buys a portfolio of stocks and then writes or sells call options in order to generate income at earning options premium. And so these strategies can offer the capital appreciation of equities, but with more income and a little bit of a smoother ride.

So we call it lower highs and higher lows. And this type of profile can make a lot of sense for income-seeking investors. And so GPIQ specifically is designed to deliver lower volatility exposure to the NASDAQ 100 Index while also providing consistent monthly distributions with an income stream that's diversifying from traditional fixed income.

So it's one of two of our premium income ETFs. The other is GPIX, which offers the same – this value proposition, but with exposure to the S&P 500 Index. And so when we think about it, it's a solution for investors who want exposure to the NASDAQ Index, but they may – might want slightly lower data in addition to higher cash flow.

And so since inception, GPIQ has provided 10.5% annualized distribution on a monthly basis. And importantly, since inception through April, GPIQ has also captured about 85% of the total return of the NASDAQ Index. And so the way that it does this is through buying a portfolio of stocks that tracks the NASDAQ 100 Index, that's the buy, part of the buy-write, and the buy part is passive. And so we think investors appreciate the transparency of having exposure to this very familiar index.

But then, as I said, we're writing or selling call options, selling some potential upside in exchange for bringing in cash flow in the form of premium income. And so we think it can be a great solution for both non-taxable and taxable income-seeking investors in a non-taxable account for older investors, for example, maybe they've been holding a NASDAQ tracking ETF for years, but as they age, they want to maintain exposure and reduce some volatility and increase income.

So we think it's a great solution because it's a clean swap. In other words, it's very similar exposure, just lower volatility and higher cash flow at 10.5%. And obviously, for a non-taxable account, there'd be no tax consequences of making this switch. And GPIX offers that similar value prop for S&P 500 investors. And then for taxable accounts, it's also a great solution because the majority of the cashflow has been distributed tax-efficiently.

DS: There's a lot to unpack there, obviously, but the premium income suite, specifically going into GPIQ, I think, it's only expense ratio of 29 basis points, if I remember correctly, off the top of my head here, which is less than your competitors, which I won't talk about too much, right? But when you're looking at this, I mean…

SS: Feel free to talk about it.

DS: …investors are always looking at the expense ratio, right? And they want to see, okay, well, is this the fund for me and how much am I paying out and everything else? But as you mentioned, reducing volatility, maybe you could elaborate a little bit more on that and how that actually helps in their portfolio. Like how do you actually reduce the volatility by utilizing this fund?

SS: Yeah. So because it is an options writing strategy, we are buying a portfolio of stocks that looks a lot, or it looks like the NASDAQ 100 Index, but the act of selling call options in a down market, you can think about it as providing cushion on the downside, right?

So we're bringing in that premium income by selling calls and in a down or even flattish market, that premium income is going to cushion that down market. But then what we're doing is we are selling upside, right? We're capping or selling upside and sort of capping some of the return of the market if the market is incredibly strong. And so we're going to have exposure to the NASDAQ market, but just with those lower highs and higher lows, so slightly lower volatility.

Now, that doesn't mean that the fund has low volatility on an absolute basis. It's still growth equity. It's lower with maybe a beta of about 0.8 to the NASDAQ 100 index.

DS: Yeah, thank you for clarifying that. Now, obviously people hear about selling options at strikes above and that sort of thing. They may not understand. How do you choose those strikes where the options get sold? Is that on a monthly basis, a quarterly basis? How do you approach it within this fund?

SS: Yeah. So when we think about, we – what we're generally doing is we're choosing at the money strikes for options and we're selling monthly options but on a weekly basis. And so the reason that we choose at the money strikes for the options, one is upside. So at the money options generally cost more, right, than out of the money options.

So as a seller, you get more premium for a given level of coverage. So you can cover less and leave more of the portfolio uncovered to fully participate in market upside. Out of the money options are cheaper. So as a seller, you'd have to cover more upside for the same amount of premium.

The other benefit is tax efficiency. So in a rising market, at the money options are more likely to result in realized losses versus out of the money options. And this can help reduce the fund's net capital gain and increase the tax efficiency of the distribution.

So even though at the money options are more likely to go against the seller versus out of the money options, remember because at the money options are more valuable, as a seller, we're able to leave more of the portfolio uncovered for full market upside participation during rising markets versus using out of the money options and so generally tend to capture more upside. And this dynamic approach, we think, is a huge component of our differentiated approach.

So we think that the – where – we're trying to be active, we're trying to be active where it counts, right? We're trying to deliver passive exposure from the long equity component, again, delivering exposure to that familiar and transparent NASDAQ 100 Index, but we're active where it counts on the dynamic options writing component because we're adjusting the coverage ratio, we're adjusting how much of the portfolio’s upside we're covering or selling based on the market volatility environment.

So market volatility impacts the value of options. Higher volatility generally makes options more valuable or more expensive, and lower volatility makes options generally cheaper, right? And so as a seller, during a higher volatility market environment, we can actually bring in a certain level of premium, let's say, 10.5% for GPIQ. We can cover or sell less of the portfolio's upside, versus in a lower volatility environment, we may have to cover or sell more of the portfolio's upside.

And so generally, we – the range of the part of the portfolio that we're covering ranges between 25% and 75%. And in this most recent bout of market volatility that we just experienced, we were covering a lower part of the markets or of the portfolio's upside. And this was really important because during the rebound, because we were only covering a lower percentage of the portfolio's upside, during the rebound, our portfolio captured a lot of that market's upside.

DS: So I want to double click here and go really in for the people that are watching right now to make sure that the audience understands like, is this fun for me, right? So you're talking about this process of paying this almost 10.5% yield on a monthly occurrence, but you're actually selling those weekly option strikes to help fund that additional alpha, if you will, the cash distribution, correct?

So for the investors that are listening right now thinking, is this my portfolio? Can you maybe talk about the distribution angle versus the long-term total gain angle and kind of clarify that for them?

SS: Yeah, sure. That's actually a really important component. So this is something that income-seeking investors should be paying a lot of attention to, is not just that headline distribution rate, but also what is the total return of the fund?

And so the distribution rate is a component of total return. But when you think about it, think about like a 10% distribution. Let's say a client has $100 of NAV of the fund. A 10% distribution is going to be at $10 of distribution, right? But then let's say the client's NAV declines to $80. Well, a 10% distribution on an $80 NAV is going to be $8.

And so when you have these funds that are distributing a high - a percentage distribution, what you really have to be thinking about is the total return, because what you don't want is a declining NAV and that distribution to erode the client's capital base because that just means the dollar value of the distribution is also going to erode.

And so when you look at the headline distribution rate, that's important. But what's also important is the fund's ability to maintain or even grow the client's NAV, right? You don't want that capital base to erode. We sometimes think about this as melting ice cube effect. And so, like, the important thing for these income distributing equity funds is the high distribution rate.

Sure, that's incredibly important. And from GPIQ, from our perspective, the consistency and stability of that distribution is really important. So we've been able to deliver that 10.5% distribution annualized on a monthly basis very stably since the inception of the fund, but also really important is being able to capture that market upside, being able to maintain and also have potential for capital appreciation of the equity markets, so that ice cube doesn't melt and the client's distribution doesn't decline.

DS: I'm glad you mentioned that because there are those funds out there that are kind of like those hidden ice cubes just melting away trying to sell well that is the perfect fund for them and it doesn't actually do anything. Let's be honest.

So thank you for that. I want to wrap up here with one last question. You're at Goldman Sachs. So everybody is watching the income space that we're talking about here today, whether it's the NASDAQ 100, the S&P 500. There's all these ETFs that are out there. I'm just kind of curious, what is the firms or maybe your personal stance on where this specific part of the ETF landscape is headed from here?

SS: Yeah. So we think that derivative income ETFs, they are continuing to see really strong interest. I think it was about $22 billion in net flows year-to-date. And actually in Q1, it was the fifth highest flow category across all active and passive mutual funds and ETFs.

And so we think these funds are going to continue to garner a lot of interest as the population ages. Investors are increasingly looking to – they want income and they also want to invest in equity markets to maintain that capital appreciation potential, but with the lower highs and higher lows.

And so, we think that that interest is here now. And then additionally, with educational outlets like Seeking Alpha shining a spotlight on these strategies, we see interest only continuing to grow. But that being said, income is not the only thing that investors are trying to solve for.

So we're always evaluating the market dynamics and exploring opportunities to deliver other solutions or outcome-oriented strategies and active strategies to address a spectrum of needs across equities and fixed income. And so, in this sort of crowded landscape, I think, we want to make sure that our investors understand that we're differentiated in our approach.

So, first, I think, that the consistency and stability of the distribution is really important, right, again, 10.5% every month since inception on an annualized basis. Second is the strong market upside capture. So again, 85% of the NASDAQ Index, the total return of the NASDAQ Index since inception. And both of these are direct results of our dynamic options strategy.

And then finally, as I mentioned, that the tax efficiency of our distribution, we think that's a huge differentiator as well. In fact, of the 10.5% distribution, 9.75% is actually the after-tax distribution. So GPIQ investors have gotten to keep more of what they earn about 92% of the distribution, even after-tax.

And so we think that this fund is differentiated in the landscape. And so our philosophy is to continue to put out differentiated solutions-oriented ETFs in this landscape that helps meet investors’ needs.

DS: Sirion, music in my ears, 9.5% after-tax. We all keep an eye on taxes, let's be honest.

So thank you so much, Sirion Skulpone, everyone. This has been another great episode of ETF Spotlight. The fund, again, was GPIQ from Goldman Sachs, tracking the NASDAQ 100, giving you that monthly distribution as well. Go ahead and check it out here on Seeking Alpha or anywhere across the internet. And we'll see you next week here back on ETF Spotlight. Take care.

data.title: Diageo: Outlook Confirmed - We Remain Buyers
data.description:
Last time, we asked the question: 'Half Full or Half Empty?' Following the Q3 2025 trading update and Guinness Investor Day, we now have our answer. In a challenging moment and a period of a lack of topline growth, Diageo's (NYSE:DEO) (OTCPK:DGEAF) message was clear. The company will control margins and cash flow, expecting to deliver positive operating leverage in all scenarios in the Fiscal Year 2026. Our team reported potential tariffs' impact on US sales and destocking in LatAm; however, we still see a potential valuation upside due to 1) a valuation discount compared to the historical average, 2) premiumization and supply chain optimizations strategy, and 3) supportive capital allocation priorities.

Fig 1

Q3 Results and Our Positive Take

Rapidly commenting on the company's Q3 trading update, Diageo delivered net sales by 2.9% to $4.4 billion. This was supported by positive volume and price MIX, while FX was unfavourable and disposals negatively impacted the company. Excluding APAC, Diageo reported growth in all regions (Fig. 2). Asia Pacific was negatively affected by continued consumer downgrading.

Source: Diageo Q3 results ppt - Fig 2

Why are we positive?

  1. As anticipated in our previous analysis, "H2 organic sales and EBIT are expected to improve." The company is on track to deliver on its H2 outlook with a "sequential improvement vs. H1" (Fig. 4). This results in a four-basis-point increase in sales, supported by the strong launch of Crown Royal Blackberry. So related to 2025, the company reiterated its full-year guidance on net sales and EBIT. More importantly, looking ahead, Diageo continues to see operating leverage, with its core operating profit growth ahead of sales. Despite seeing topline uncertainty from the USA and APAC, Europe is performing well, and with lower input costs, Diageo should deliver EPS growth.
  2. Compared to an uncertain estimate, the company disclosed a potential US tariff impact (Fig. 5). Assuming 10% tariffs remain, there is a $150 million annualised cost to assume. Before pricing, the company expects to mitigate approximately 50% of the impact. This will be supported by cost management and re-allocation of CAPEX. This is a soft landing scenario, so we positively report this update. Additionally, Diageo FX should be a slight benefit in Fiscal Year 2026, given the fact that the company is already hedged at an 80% level.
  3. Diageo also announced a $500 million cost savings programme for the next three years (Fig. 3). This will enable reinvestment in growth opportunities and improve its operating leverage. According to Diageo, this will support a $3 billion FCF from next year, bringing down the company's leverage to a 2.5- 3x range no later than Fiscal Year 2028. There are no estimates of disposal; however, we anticipate $3 billion in free cash flow as a supportive floor from next year and might accelerate over time. Looking back, FCF delivery has been inconsistent, and 2026 CAPEX will be elevated. That said, Diageo might unlock new cost savings and lower working capital requirements. Overall, there are three primary targets: 1) lower A&P expenses, 2) lower overheads, and 3) a better supply chain with an efficient plan and a new agility program. Related to the latest point, there will be an update in August. Even if we are not accounting for disposal, Digeo has profitable assets, and we see opportunities for portfolio simplification and trimming.

Fig 3

Fig 4

Fig 5

Earnings Change and Valuation

Although the Global Spirits market will likely experience another challenging year, Diageo's LATAM recovery (Fig. 2) and manageable USA tariff impact are key points to note. Aside from a free cash flow target of $3 billion, there are positive headlines about the Don Julio and Guinness outperformance in the USA market. Assuming no sales growth, we believe the company will deliver a positive core operating profit increase. Therefore, rolling forward our financials estiamtes, Diageo topline sales are estimated at $20.2 billion with a core operating profit of $6 billion. In our assessment, we have also considered the benefits of lower cost. Growth in Tequila and favourable COGS trends should support operating leverage. We believe we are below Diageo's 2026 guidance. So, according to the P&L projections, our earnings are set at $3.6 billion, serving an earnings per share of $6.48 in ADR and £1.61. This assumes an outstanding share of 2.23 billion, while Diageo plc has approximately 555.57 million American Depositary Receipts (ADRs).

Diageo is a globally diversified drinks player, with an estimated 30% share of the global premium spirits market. Therefore, the company should trade at a premium valuation. This is no longer the case. For consistency reasons, we continue to value the company with an unchanged P/E target of 18.5x, so with a lower EPS, our valuation moved from £30.5 to £29.8 ($148.8 in ADR), keeping a buy rating. This assumes FX rate = 1.25 USD/GBP. The company's current valuation is also lower than its historical average and the FWD P/E sector median valuation (Fig. 6).

Potential upside does not include: 1) the UK-India free trade agreement with a supportive opportunity ahead for Scotch growth, 2) positive FX development, 3) higher-than-anticipated cost savings, and 4) multiple re-rating.

Fig 6

Risks

Downside risks include: 1) FX changes in sales and earnings, and Diageo is particularly sensitive to the US$/£ volatility, 2) turnover is subject to consumer variances (but its broad portfolio very much helps the company earnings), 3) higher regulatory headwinds such as lower marketing, anti-conspicuous alcohol consumption worldwide, and additional excise duty, 4) tariffs development, 5) Wall Street analysts earnings downside revision, 6) long term growth, and 7) valuation multiple. The company is also exposed to poor weather conditions, supply chain shocks, and COGS price changes.

Conclusion

Investor positioning on the spirits market has turned negative; however, Diageo's broad-based portfolio is attractive. The company is also managing its costs and has reassured a positive 2026 outlook. USA tariffs seem now manageable, and even applying lower estimates, the company is still a buy.

data.title: Lazard Opportunistic Strategies Portfolio Q1 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Market Overview

Equity markets worldwide receded in the first quarter, as the outlooks for corporate spending and global economic growth were affected by US tariff plans.

The quarter proved to be a turbulent one for global stock markets. All eyes were on the US, with initial investor enthusiasm for the administration’s anticipated business-friendly policies tempered by numerous statements and administration actions that had investors debating their ramifications. With surveys indicating lower business and household confidence leading up to US tariff deadline on 2 April, investors were wary that the uncertainty might lead to a pull-back in corporate and consumer spending, lowering economic growth. The market was also affected by the news that Chinese startup DeepSeek had developed a lower-cost artificial intelligence (AI) model that performed comparably to those created by its US Big Tech rivals, bringing AI-exposed companies broadly under pressure.

Concerns about a global trade war and its potential impact on the world economy was a significant risk factor considered by major central banks in their monetary policy decisions. With price pressures persisting, the US Federal Reserve held interest rates steady during the quarter in anticipation of higher inflation and slower growth. The European Central Bank (ECB) reduced interest rates by 25 basis points at its policy meetings in January and March and noted that the “disinflation process remained on track” in the eurozone. Likewise, the central banks in England, Sweden, and Switzerland each reduced interest rates by 25 bps during the quarter. Meanwhile, in Asia, the Bank of Japan (BOJ) held interest rates steady, citing concerns about the potential impact global trade tensions would have on Japan’s export-reliant economy.

Against this backdrop, equity markets in the developed world fell in the quarter while those in the developing world rose. In the US, stocks declined, as concerns about tariff risks and a sell-off of companies leveraged to AI weighed on the market. European stocks outperformed, driven by a combination of rate cuts and the enthusiasm for defense contractor stocks. In emerging markets, stocks in China advanced, as investors bid up shares of Chinese internet companies after the AI breakthrough of a Chinese start-up. Conversely, stocks in Taiwan fell on concerns over technology industry trade.

Increased uncertainty triggered heightened risk aversion, resulting in mixed performance across global bond markets. With investors seeking safe-haven assets, the US government bond market rallied, with the yield on the benchmark 10-year Treasury note 36 basis points (bps) lower than three months earlier, ending the quarter at 4.21%. Real yields, as measured by Treasury Inflation-Protected Securities (TIPS), also declined, falling 24 bps to 1.85%. In contrast, European bond markets faced headwinds as Germany's fiscal stimulus raised term premia. The yield on the 10-year German Bund rose 37 bps to close the quarter at 2.74% while the price of government bonds from Italy and France bonds also weakened, with their yields climbing 35 bps and 26 bps, respectively.

Elsewhere in the sovereign debt market, the yield on 10-year government bonds from the UK and China edged higher, climbing 11 bps and 15 bps, respectively. Meanwhile, Japanese government bonds underperformed significantly, with 10-year yields rising 40 bps, due primarily to higher domestic inflation stemming from wage increases. Across most markets, short-dated bonds fared better, leading to a general steepening of yield curves. Despite the rise in risk aversion, credit markets proved more resilient than might have been expected under such conditions.

Credit spreads widened only modestly and remain at levels historically inconsistent with recessionary scenarios. Meanwhile, emerging markets debt recorded a solid performance, both in hard currency and local currency terms. Much of the repricing related to the new tariff regime had already occurred last summer, allowing these markets to weather recent volatility relatively well.

Portfolio Review

In the first quarter, the Lazard Opportunistic Strategies Portfolio’s Institutional Shares fell 0.69%, while Open Shares retreated 0.79%. This is compared to the 0.04% gain of the Global Asset Allocation Blended Index [1] and the 1.79% depreciation of the MSCI World Index. (Portfolio returns are measured net of fees and in US dollar terms.)

Diversifying investments, which represented 33.8% of the Portfolio at the end of the period, outperformed the MSCI World Index, the Global Asset Allocation Blended Index, and US fixed income, as represented by the Bloomberg US Aggregate Bond Index.

Exposure to the Portfolio’s European defense basket, long-term US Treasuries, and copper contributed, while exposure to the Portfolio’s global high-conviction basket detracted.

Trending investments, which represented 40.5% of the Portfolio at quarter-end, underperformed the MSCI World Index, the Global Asset Allocation Blended Index, and US fixed income. Exposure to Japanese equities contributed, while exposure to US growth and US large cap detracted.

Contrarian investments, which represented 25.8% of the Portfolio at quarter end, underperformed the MSCI World Index, the Global Asset Allocation Blended Index, and US fixed income. The top contributor was exposure to the eurozone, while exposure to tech-software and US small cap equities detracted.

[1] The Global Asset Allocation Blended Index is a blended index constructed by the Portfolio’s Investment Manager, composed of 60% MSCI World Index and 40% Bloomberg US Aggregate Bond Index, rebalanced quarterly.

Outlook

At the end of the first quarter, risks to the outlook appear greater than at the beginning of the year. Even then, we were concerned that headline data overstated the strength of underlying conditions and that the potential for significant US policy change posed downside risks. Since then, policy change has been faster and bigger than anticipated, and markets have begun to reflect growth concerns. In the first quarter, the S&P 500 Index was down more than 4.5% (with global indices outperforming), and the 10-year US Treasury yield fell to roughly 4.2% from a peak of nearly 4.8%. If growth concerns mount, equities seem likely to decline further; however, there is more ambiguity about interest rates.

Tariffs are widely thought to push growth down and prices up. Prior to the COVID-19 pandemic, the impact on inflation was believed to be temporary and largely outside the control of central banks, which might ultimately need to cut interest rates to support growth if the impacts were large (instead of hiking to fight inflation). However, the pandemic-driven spike in inflation creates greater uncertainty now because: (a) pre-pandemic models underestimated the impact of a large shock to supply chains and

(b) consumers and businesses may now be more sensitive to inflation and thus more likely to change price-setting behavior. At a minimum, central banks will likely need to “wait and see” how tariffs affect supply chains and inflation expectations before reacting to any tariff-induced slowdown.

Much uncertainty also remains about the size and nature of potential policy change, and how it will affect the global outlook, with risks in either direction. We believe recent developments argue for reducing risk and adding defense. However, we also believe that additional interest rate volatility is likely.

The values of these securities may be affected by changes in currency rates, application of a country’s specific tax laws, changes in government administration, and economic and monetary policy. The securities markets of emerging markets countries can be extremely volatile; performance can also be influenced by political, social, and economic factors affecting companies in these countries.

An investment in bonds carries risk. If interest rates rise, bond prices usually decline. The longer a bond’s maturity, the greater the impact a change in interest rates can have on its price. If you do not hold a bond until maturity, you may experience a gain or loss when you sell. Bonds also carry the risk of default, which is the risk that the issuer is unable to make further income and principal payments.
